tools.name: add-to-context
tools.description:
Adds the object to the chat context.
Use this it for objects that are useful long-term for the conversation.

obj.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: remove-from-context
tools.description:
Removes the object from the chat context.
Use this it for objects that are no longer useful for the conversation.

obj.description:

- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: load-object
tools.description:
Loads the object or relation content.
Can load multiple objects at at time.
Use the to read the data when you have a DXN.
Call this tool with an array of one or more DXNs or object IDs.
When use see a reference ({ '/': 'dxn:...' }), you can call this function to load the object.
Note that returned data is only a snapshot in time, and might have changed since the object was last loaded.

items.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: create-object
tools.description:
Creates a new object and adds it to the current space.
Get the schema from the schema-list tool and ensure that the data matches the corresponding schema.
References are provided in the following format: { "/": "dxn:..." }.
Reference examples: { "/": "dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4" }, { "/": "dxn:queue:data:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4" }

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

Let me first check what schemas are available for organizations.

key.description: Unique registration key for the blueprint

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

concurrency.description: Maximum number of concurrent invocations of the trigger. For Feed triggers, this will process Feed items in parallel.
concurrency.title: Concurrency

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

  <dxn>dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTA8</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.organization</typename>
</object>
Create an organization called "Cyberdyne Systems".

Let me first check what schemas are available for organizations.

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

Note that returned data is only a snapshot in time, and might have changed since the object was last loaded.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }

content.text: Let me summarize the available schemas/typenames.

Here are all the available schemas and their typenames:

| # | Typename | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | `com.example.type.expando` | A flexible/generic object type that allows arbitrary additional properties. |
| 2 | `org.dxos.type.function` | Represents a function/blueprint with input/output schemas, versioning, and service bindings. |
| 3 | `org.dxos.type.organization` | An organization with fields like name, description, status, image, website, and location. |

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>
List all available schemas. Tell me what typenames are available.

content.text: The user wants to see all available schemas and their typenames.

key.description: Unique registration key for the blueprint

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

concurrency.description: Maximum number of concurrent invocations of the trigger. For Feed triggers, this will process Feed items in parallel.
concurrency.title: Concurrency

content.text: Let me summarize the available schemas/typenames.

Here are all the available schemas and their typenames:

| # | Typename | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | `com.example.type.expando` | A flexible/generic object type that allows arbitrary additional properties. |
| 2 | `org.dxos.type.function` | Represents a function/blueprint with input/output schemas, versioning, and service bindings. |
| 3 | `org.dxos.type.organization` | An organization with fields like name, description, status, image, website, and location. |
| 4 | `org.dxos.type.person` | A person/contact with rich fields including full name, emails, phone numbers, addresses, URLs, job title, organization, and more. |
| 5 | `org.dxos.type.tag` | A simple tag with a label and optional hue (color), used for tagging/labeling other objects. |
| 6 | `org.dxos.type.trigger` | A trigger configuration that can fire functions based on events like emails, queues, subscriptions, timers (cron), or webhooks. |

These are the six typenames currently available in your space. Let me know if you'd like more details on any specific schema or want to create/query objects of a particular type!

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>
List all available schemas. Tell me what typenames are available.

content.text: The user wants to see all available schemas and their typenames.

key.description: Unique registration key for the blueprint

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

concurrency.description: Maximum number of concurrent invocations of the trigger. For Feed triggers, this will process Feed items in parallel.
concurrency.title: Concurrency

content.text: Let me summarize the available schemas/typenames.

Here are all the available schemas and their typenames:

| # | Typename | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | `com.example.type.expando` | A flexible/generic object type that allows arbitrary additional properties. |
| 2 | `org.dxos.type.function` | Represents a function/blueprint with input/output schemas, versioning, and service bindings. |
| 3 | `org.dxos.type.organization` | An organization with fields like name, description, status, image, website, and location. |
| 4 | `org.dxos.type.person` | A person/contact with rich fields including full name, emails, phone numbers, addresses, URLs, job title, organization, and more. |
| 5 | `org.dxos.type.tag` | A simple tag with a label and optional hue (color), used for tagging/labeling other objects. |
| 6 | `org.dxos.type.trigger` | A trigger configuration that can fire functions based on events like emails, queues, subscriptions, timers (cron), or webhooks. |

These are the six typenames currently available in your space. Let me know if you'd like more details on any specific schema or want to create/query objects of a particular type!

params.success:
# DXOS — The Decentralized Operating System

## Overview

<cite index="1-3">DXOS is an open source framework for building real-time, collaborative web applications that run entirely on the client and communicate peer-to-peer, without the need for centralized servers.</cite> The name stands for **Decentralized Operating System**. <cite index="9-3">DXOS is an independent public organization whose mission is to provide open source technologies that allow for a free and open decentralized internet.</cite>

## Company Details

- **Founded:** <cite index="2-5">DXOS was founded in 2021.</cite>
- **Headquarters:** <cite index="2-6">DXOS is headquartered in New York, NY.</cite>
- **Status:** <cite index="2-8">DXOS is a Private company.</cite>
- **Industry:** <cite index="2-7">DXOS's primary industry is Software Development Applications.</cite>
- **Investors:** <cite index="2-10">Backed VC, BlueYard Capital, Empty Set Group, and PL Capital (United States) have invested in DXOS.</cite>
- **License:** Open-source under the MIT license.
- **Related Entity:** <cite index="28-3,28-4">Braneframe is a Web3 company building peer-to-peer privacy-preserving applications built on open-source protocols. It is a leading project in the DXOS (Decentralized Operating System) ecosystem, whose participants are working toward an alternative to the tech giant-dominated public cloud.</cite>

## Mission & Philosophy

<cite index="2-1">DXOS is a developer of a decentralized platform designed to create the first functionally and commercially competitive, fully decentralized alternative to the corporate cloud.</cite> <cite index="2-4">The company offers a network operating system, infrastructure platform, programming framework, and toolchain, enabling businesses to develop privacy-preserving applications for its users and a fairer marketplace for open-source developers.</cite>

<cite index="1-21,1-22">They're setting a new standard for how modern software is built by reducing reliance on centralized servers and focusing on scalable peer-to-peer data networks. Their framework empowers anyone to create real-time collaborative tools within a privacy-focused, local-first workspace that is enhanced by AI capabilities.</cite>

## Core Technology Stack

The DXOS SDK includes three primary protocols/components:

### 1. ECHO (Data Layer)
<cite index="1-6">ECHO is an open source database architecture that incorporates transparent data replication and conflict resolution for secure and scalable local storage.</cite> <cite index="12-8">It serves as a peer-to-peer database and reactive state container for offline-first, real-time, collaborative apps.</cite>

### 2. HALO (Identity Layer)
<cite index="1-7">HALO seamlessly manages digital identities using public/private key pairs allowing nuanced control over access to applications, databases, and networked devices.</cite> <cite index="4-5">HALO is a decentralized identity and access control protocol that makes it easier to build collaborative applications.</cite>

### 3. MESH (Network Layer)
<cite index="1-9">MESH extends existing internet protocols to enable secure and resilient peer-to-peer networks without the need for centralized server infrastructure.</cite>

### CRDT & Automerge
<cite index="1-11,1-12">DXOS leverages Automerge — a powerful CRDT that enables multiple concurrent users to operate on the same data without loss or conflict.</cite>

## Architecture & How It Works

<cite index="1-14,1-15">The only reason you need a central server is to serve your application shell. Everything else happens in the client and works peer-to-peer.</cite>

- <cite index="1-17">User identity, data storage, synchronization and networking are all handled natively using the DXOS client API.</cite>
- <cite index="1-19">Data is automatically synchronized across networked devices using WebRTC for cloudless peer-to-peer networking.</cite>
- <cite index="4-6">There are still signaling servers for peer discovery, but all data exchange happens peer-to-peer, and no servers have access to the data.</cite>
- <cite index="20-11">The platform wraps its three components together in a set of SDKs (currently, React and TypeScript) that enable developers to deliver sophisticated applications quickly.</cite>

## Flagship Application: Composer

<cite index="12-4">To show off the power of DXOS, they've built Composer: a local-first workspace that is designed for small team collaboration.</cite>

<cite index="31-24">Composer blends the power of popular file formats (docs, sheets, sketch & tables) while allowing you to build plugins, deploy custom functions and leverage modern LLMs.</cite>

Key Composer features include:
- <cite index="31-25">Realtime collaboration inside of a modern markdown editor.</cite>
- <cite index="31-27">Threaded comments across multiple document formats.</cite>
- <cite index="31-29">Custom plugins to enhance functionality.</cite>
- <cite index="31-31">Custom schemas to work with structured data.</cite>
- <cite index="31-33">A local-first spreadsheet that supports custom functions.</cite>
- <cite index="31-6">Machine learning to build agentic workflows that enhance team productivity while protecting intellectual property.</cite>
- <cite index="31-13">Custom agents that use Composer data as a dynamic context window.</cite>

<cite index="40-1,40-2">Composer is built with extensibility at its core. Everything within the application is built using the same App Framework APIs, from the UI down to data and business logic; anything in Composer can be customized and enhanced.</cite>

### Privacy in Composer
<cite index="15-7,15-8">Composer is built on the peer-to-peer protocols of DXOS which are designed to be private by default. The data is stored on your device and only shared with others you explicitly invite.</cite>

## DXOS Agents

<cite index="38-3,38-4,38-5,38-6">The DXOS Agent provides offline backup and synchronization capabilities. It continuously replicates all data from all browser sessions. When browser sessions are offline, it will replicate any changes from spaces to any peers in the space. This allows changes to a space to be available even if the other peer was offline when the change was made.</cite>

<cite index="32-1,32-2">Agents are DXOS instances running without an attached user interface. A common use of agents is to run on a server and provide availability if all other devices are offline, but this is completely optional.</cite>

<cite index="4-2">DXOS is launching a fully-managed service to host agents.</cite> <cite index="33-1,33-2">Their vision is to enable significant savings through the interoperability of DXOS applications. Local-first apps can share the same agent and data storage, maintaining data privacy without extra charges for each additional app.</cite>

## Community & Development

- <cite index="3-2">DXOS has 64 repositories available on GitHub.</cite>
- <cite index="6-4">The main repository is a TypeScript implementation of the DXOS protocols, SDK, toolchain and Composer.</cite>
- <cite index="7-6">DXOS was created by a small core team of developers from many countries.</cite>
- <cite index="1-23">The DXOS Community is made of a diverse group of open source developers from all over the world.</cite>
- <cite index="9-4,9-5">DXOS.org is a decentralized global community of open source developers. The community is organized via an independent foundation (DXOS.foundation), which is funded by members of the community.</cite>

## Key Differentiators

1. **Local-first:** Applications work offline and store data locally on user devices.
2. **Peer-to-peer:** No centralized servers required for data exchange; uses WebRTC.
3. **Privacy-preserving:** Data never passes through third-party servers.
4. **Real-time collaboration:** Powered by CRDTs (Automerge) for conflict-free concurrent editing.
5. **Fully open source:** MIT licensed, with transparent development.
6. **Extensible:** Plugin architecture (especially in Composer) allows deep customization.
7. **AI-enhanced:** Support for local AI models and agentic workflows.

## Links

- **Website:** https://dxos.org
- **Documentation:** https://docs.dxos.org
- **GitHub:** https://github.com/dxos
- **Blog:** https://blog.dxos.org
- **Composer:** https://dxos.org/composer

Note that returned data is only a snapshot in time, and might have changed since the object was last loaded.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>echo://BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U/01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTAB</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.organization</typename>
</object>
Create an organization called "Cyberdyne Systems".

content.text: Let me first check what schemas are available to find the right type for an organization.
Let me first check the available schemas to find the right type for an organization.

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

concurrency.description: Maximum number of concurrent invocations of the trigger. For Feed triggers, this will process Feed items in parallel.
concurrency.title: Concurrency

details like a description, status, website, or location if you'd like.

Loads the object or relation content.
Can load multiple objects at at time.
Use the to read the data when you have a DXN.
Call this tool with an array of one or more DXNs or object IDs.
When use see a reference ({ '/': 'dxn:...' }), you can call this function to load the object.
Note that returned data is only a snapshot in time, and might have changed since the object was last loaded.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>echo://BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U/01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTAB</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.organization</typename>
</object>

<object>
  <dxn>echo://BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U/01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTC2</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.person</typename>
</object>
Create an organization called "Cyberdyne Systems".

content.text: Let me first check what schemas are available to find the right type for an organization.
Let me first check the available schemas to find the right type for an organization.

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

concurrency.description: Maximum number of concurrent invocations of the trigger. For Feed triggers, this will process Feed items in parallel.
concurrency.title: Concurrency

</blueprint>
Search for all organizations. How many are there?

content.text: Let me search for organizations in the space. I need to find the right typename first. Let me list schemas to find the organization type, and also query for organizations.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

concurrency.description: Maximum number of concurrent invocations of the trigger. For Feed triggers, this will process Feed items in parallel.
concurrency.title: Concurrency

1. **Acme Corp**
2. **Globex Industries

Call this tool with an array of one or more DXNs or object IDs.

Note that returned data is only a snapshot in time, and might have changed since the object was last loaded.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.

List all available schemas. Tell me what typenames are available.

content.text: The user wants to see all available schemas. Let me list them.

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

concurrency.description: Maximum number of concurrent invocations of the trigger. For Feed triggers, this will process Feed items in parallel.
concurrency.title: Concurrency

response.delta: me organize the schemas nicely for the user.

Here are all the available schemas and their typenames:

|

generic object type that allows arbitrary additional properties. |
| 2 | **`
org.dxos.type.function`** | Represents a function definition with name, description, input/output schemas, services

, and bindings. |
| 3 | **`org.dxos.type.organization`** | An organization with fields like name

, description, status (prospect/qualified/active/commit/reject), image, website, and location. |
| 4 | **`org.dxos.type.person`** | A person/contact
with extensive fields: full name, preferred name, nickname, image, employer (reference to

organization), job title, department, emails, phone numbers, addresses, URLs, birthday, and more. |
| 5 | **`org.dxos.type.tag`** | A tag with
a label and optional hue (color). Used for tagging/lab

eling other objects. |
| 6 | **`org.dxos.type.trigger`** | A trigger definition that links
to a function and can be configured with various trigger types: email, queue, subscription, timer (cron), or webhook. |
Let me know if you'd like more details on any specific schema or want to create/query
objects of any of these types!

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>
List all available schemas. Tell me what typenames are available.

content.text: The user wants to see all available schemas. Let me list them.

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

concurrency.description: Maximum number of concurrent invocations of the trigger. For Feed triggers, this will process Feed items in parallel.
concurrency.title: Concurrency

content.text: Let me organize the schemas nicely for the user.

Here are all the available schemas and their typenames:

| # | Typename | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **`com.example.type.expando`** | A flexible/generic object type that allows arbitrary additional properties. |
| 2 | **`org.dxos.type.function`** | Represents a function definition with name, description, input/output schemas, services, and bindings. |
| 3 | **`org.dxos.type.organization`** | An organization with fields like name, description, status (prospect/qualified/active/commit/reject), image, website, and location. |
| 4 | **`org.dxos.type.person`** | A person/contact with extensive fields: full name, preferred name, nickname, image, employer (reference to organization), job title, department, emails, phone numbers, addresses, URLs, birthday, and more. |
| 5 | **`org.dxos.type.tag`** | A tag with a label and optional hue (color). Used for tagging/labeling other objects. |
| 6 | **`org.dxos.type.trigger`** | A trigger definition that links to a function and can be configured with various trigger types: email, queue, subscription, timer (cron), or webhook. |

Let me know if you'd like more details on any specific schema or want to create/query objects of any of these types!

| # | Typename | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **`com.example.type.expando`** | A flexible/generic object type that allows arbitrary additional properties. |
| 2 | **`org.dxos.type.function`** | Represents a function definition with name, description, input/output schemas, services, and bindings. |
| 3 | **`org.dxos.type.organization`** | An organization with fields like name, description, status (prospect/qualified/active/commit/reject), image, website, and location. |
| 4 | **`org.dxos.type.person`** | A person/contact with extensive fields: full name, preferred name, nickname, image, employer (reference to organization), job title, department, emails, phone numbers, addresses, URLs, birthday, and more. |
| 5 | **`org.dxos.type.tag`** | A tag with a label and optional hue (color). Used for tagging/labeling other objects. |
| 6 | **`org.dxos.type.trigger`** | A trigger definition that links to a function and can be configured with various trigger types: email, queue, subscription, timer (cron), or webhook. |

Let me know if you'd like more details on any specific schema or want to create/query objects of any of these types!

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

params.success:
## DXOS — Research Summary

### Overview

<cite index="5-1">DXOS is the Decentralized Operating System — a platform for building fully decentralized applications.</cite> <cite index="1-3">DXOS is an open source framework for building real-time, collaborative web applications that run entirely on the client and communicate peer-to-peer, without the need for centralized servers.</cite> <cite index="1-21,1-22">The project is setting a new standard for how modern software is built by reducing reliance on centralized servers and focusing on scalable peer-to-peer data networks. The framework empowers anyone to create real-time collaborative tools within a privacy-focused, local-first workspace that is enhanced by AI capabilities.</cite>

### Company Details

- <cite index="8-1">DXOS was founded in 2021.</cite>
- <cite index="8-2">DXOS is headquartered in New York, NY.</cite>
- <cite index="4-1">The company operates in the Technology, Information and Internet industry, has 11-50 employees, and is classified as a Nonprofit organization.</cite>
- <cite index="6-1">DXOS was created by a small core team of developers from many countries.</cite>
- <cite index="10-2">The team has presence in NYC, LA, and Warsaw.</cite>
- <cite index="31-5">The community includes 8 cities worldwide, 30+ contributors, and 200+ community members.</cite>

### Funding & Investors

- <cite index="21-2">Backed VC, BlueYard Capital, Empty Set Group, and PL Capital (United States) have invested in DXOS.</cite>
- <cite index="23-1">BlueYard Capital lists DXOS as a portfolio company</cite>, and <cite index="23-44">BlueYard partnered at seed, together with Backed.</cite>

### Core Technology Stack

The DXOS SDK consists of three major components that work together:

1. **ECHO (Database):** <cite index="1-6">ECHO is an open source database architecture that incorporates transparent data replication and conflict resolution for secure and scalable local storage.</cite> <cite index="12-8">It serves as a peer-to-peer database and reactive state container for offline-first, real-time, collaborative apps.</cite>

2. **HALO (Identity):** <cite index="1-7">HALO seamlessly manages digital identities using public/private key pairs allowing nuanced control over access to applications, databases, and networked devices.</cite>

3. **MESH (Networking):** <cite index="1-9">MESH extends existing internet protocols to enable secure and resilient peer-to-peer networks without the need for centralized server infrastructure.</cite>

### How It Works

- <cite index="1-11,1-12">DXOS leverages Automerge, a powerful CRDT that enables multiple concurrent users to operate on the same data without loss or conflict.</cite>
- <cite index="1-14,1-15">The only reason you need a central server is to serve your application shell. Everything else happens in the client and works peer-to-peer.</cite>
- <cite index="1-17">User identity, data storage, synchronization and networking are all handled natively using the DXOS client API.</cite>
- <cite index="1-19">Data is automatically synchronized across networked devices using WebRTC for cloudless peer-to-peer networking.</cite>
- <cite index="9-6">There are still signaling servers for peer discovery, but all data exchange happens peer-to-peer, and no servers have access to the data.</cite>

### Flagship Application: Composer

<cite index="3-6">To show off the power of DXOS, they've built Composer: a local-first workspace that is designed for small team collaboration.</cite> <cite index="5-2">DXOS is also the home of Composer — an extensible IDE, built for both real time and async collaboration.</cite>

<cite index="32-7">Composer is a collaborative productivity app where developers can build and organize knowledge, extend with custom data and UI, and run private AI against their knowledge locally.</cite>

Key principles of Composer include:
- **Extensibility:** <cite index="37-3,37-4">Composer is built with extensibility at its core. Everything within the application is built using the same App Framework APIs, from the UI down to data and business logic — anything in Composer can be customized and enhanced.</cite>
- **Collaboration:** <cite index="32-10,32-11">Composer has real-time multiplayer implemented through a local-first model, which makes a lot of things better: from meeting notes to working on airplanes.</cite>
- **Privacy:** <cite index="13-7,13-8">Composer is built on the peer-to-peer protocols of DXOS which are designed to be private by default. The data is stored on your device and only shared with others you explicitly invite.</cite>

Composer features include:
- <cite index="31-14,31-15">Blending the power of popular file formats (docs, sheets, sketch & tables) with the ability to build plugins, deploy custom functions and leverage modern LLMs, plus real-time collaboration inside a modern markdown editor.</cite>
- <cite index="31-34">Building custom agents that use Composer data as a dynamic context window.</cite>
- <cite index="31-49">Importing and exporting an entire database as plain, human-readable markdown, JSON, and media files.</cite>
- <cite index="39-1">Scripts in Composer allow you to deploy custom functions that run on the edge.</cite>

### Development Status

- <cite index="3-7,3-8">The DXOS SDK is under active development, however the current focus is building features and functionality that enables the development of Composer. To this end, they're currently only able to provide limited support to developers using the SDK.</cite>
- <cite index="32-1,32-2">Composer is not yet ready for production. There are bugs, and breaking changes may occur.</cite>
- <cite index="9-2">DXOS is also launching a fully-managed service to host agents.</cite>

### Technical Implementation

- <cite index="2-4">The codebase is a TypeScript implementation of the DXOS protocols, SDK, toolchain and Composer.</cite>
- <cite index="2-2">DXOS has 64 repositories available on GitHub.</cite>
- <cite index="14-2">The project is open-source under the MIT license.</cite>
- <cite index="36-4">The main repository shows active commit activity and was last updated in February 2026.</cite>

### Mission

<cite index="20-4">The mission at DXOS is to help developers build privacy-preserving, self-sovereign software.</cite> <cite index="6-2">Their goal is to enable developers to quickly and easily create powerful collaborative, interoperable applications.</cite>

### Links
- **Website:** https://dxos.org
- **Documentation:** https://docs.dxos.org
- **GitHub:** https://github.com/dxos/dxos
- **Blog:** https://blog.dxos.org
- **Composer:** https://dxos.org/composer
